Forced to Eat Mango (27): Setting Up MongoDB Atlas Step by Step
Next up, let me say a bit about MongoDB Atlas. This post also records how to actually sign up for MongoDB Atlas.

MongoDB Atlas is basically a cloud service for MongoDB. It lets you use MongoDB without installing it on your own machine. To make sure you don't land on the wrong site, I'd suggest clicking the official link below:
Alright, let's get our own MongoDB Atlas set up.
Once you're on the MongoDB Atlas site through the link above, look for the "Try Free" button on the screen. Click it and it'll ask you to register — if you've already got an account, just log in.
Personally I'll just log in with Google (lazy, I know).
After registering it'll ask you a few things. This page is really just the privacy terms page:
Accept Privacy Policy & Terms of Service

Accept Privacy Policy and Terms of Service
Please confirm the following terms and conditions to complete your account creation.

I accept the Privacy Policy and Terms of Service
Accept Privacy Policy and Terms of Service
Please confirm the following terms and conditions to complete your account creation.
I accept the Privacy Policy and Terms of Service
After hitting allow, you'll wait a moment for the welcome screen:
After a bit it'll ask you to fill in some info so it understands what you want to do with MongoDB Atlas:
What do you want to do with MongoDB Atlas
Let me help with these too:
Q: What is your goal today? Your answer will help us guide you to successfully getting started with MongoDB Atlas.

A: Build a new application
B: Migrate an existing application
C: Learn MongoDB
D: Explore what I can build

Q: What type of application are you building?
A: Just pick one.

Q: What is your preferred language? We'll use this to customize code samples and content we share with you. You can always change this later.
A: JavaScript, of course.
Once you're done, you get to pick the database type. Obviously go with Free here — we're just practicing after all:
After choosing the database, it's time to deploy the cloud host:
For the host, just go with AWS and Hong Kong:
After you go in, it'll need a moment, so you'll see this message at the bottom:
Once you see the screen above, that means it's been created successfully.
Next, the screen will ask you to set up a username and password:
This username and password below are really important — they're the credentials you'll use to connect to MongoDB in a bit. After adding them, you'll also see them listed below:
Next, further down, we also need to set the allowed IP, otherwise you won't be able to connect remotely. So I'd suggest hitting "Add My Current IP Address":
Once that's allowed, you can hit Finish and Close.
When you see the screen below, you're all set:
Alright, in the next post we'll get the database ready.
